passages.explanation: Jesus, full of the Holy Spirit, returns from the Jordan and is led by the Spirit into the wilderness for forty days, tempted by the devil. He eats nothing during those days, and at the end he's hungry. The devil says: if you are the Son of God, tell this stone to become bread. Jesus answers with Deuteronomy 8:3: man shall not live on bread alone. The first temptation targets legitimate need — Jesus is starving. The temptation isn't hunger itself; it's using divine power to serve himself rather than trusting the Father's provision.

The devil leads Jesus up and shows him all the kingdoms of the world in an instant. He offers all their authority and splendour: it has been given to me, and I can give it to anyone I want. If you worship me, it will all be yours. Jesus answers with Deuteronomy 6:13: worship the Lord your God and serve him only. The devil offers a shortcut to global authority — no cross required. Jesus came to rule all nations, but through suffering and resurrection, not through compromise with evil.

The devil takes Jesus to Jerusalem, sets him on the highest point of the temple, and says: if you are the Son of God, throw yourself down from here — quoting Psalm 91 about angels protecting him. Jesus answers with Deuteronomy 6:16: do not put the Lord your God to the test. When the devil finishes every temptation, he leaves Jesus until an opportune time. The devil can quote Scripture — the issue is how it's used. Testing God by manufacturing a crisis to force divine intervention isn't faith; it's manipulation. Luke notes the devil leaves temporarily, not permanently. He'll return.

Jesus returns to Galilee in the power of the Spirit. News about him spreads, he teaches in their synagogues and everyone praises him. He goes to Nazareth, where he was brought up, and on the Sabbath enters the synagogue as is his custom. He stands up to read, is handed the scroll of Isaiah, and unrolls it to Isaiah 61: the Spirit of the Lord is on me, because he has anointed me to proclaim good news to the poor. He has sent me to proclaim freedom for prisoners, recovery of sight for the blind, to set the oppressed free, to proclaim the year of the Lord's favour. He rolls up the scroll, gives it back, and sits down. Every eye in the synagogue is fixed on him. He says: today this Scripture is fulfilled in your hearing. Jesus stops reading mid-sentence — Isaiah 61:2 continues with 'and the day of vengeance of our God,' but Jesus leaves that out. The year of favour has arrived. The day of vengeance hasn't — not yet.

Everyone speaks well of him and is amazed at the gracious words coming from his mouth. But they also say: isn't this Joseph's son? Jesus reads the room: surely you'll quote the proverb — physician, heal yourself! Do here in your hometown what we heard you did in Capernaum. Then he says: truly, no prophet is accepted in his hometown. He cites two Old Testament examples: Elijah was sent not to any widow in Israel but to a widow in Sidon — a Gentile. Elisha healed not any leper in Israel but Naaman the Syrian — a Gentile. The people in the synagogue are furious. They drive him out of town to the brow of the hill to throw him off the cliff. But Jesus walks right through the crowd and goes on his way. The rage isn't about his identity — it's about the implication that God's favour extends to outsiders. Nazareth rejects Jesus because he won't stay local and exclusive.

Jesus goes to Capernaum in Galilee and teaches on the Sabbath. The people are amazed because his words have authority. In the synagogue there's a man possessed by a demon. The demon shouts: go away! What do you want with us, Jesus of Nazareth? Have you come to destroy us? I know who you are — the Holy One of God! Jesus commands sharply: be quiet! Come out of him! The demon throws the man down in front of them and comes out without injuring him. Everyone is amazed: what words these are! With authority and power he gives orders to impure spirits and they come out! News about him spreads throughout the surrounding area. The demons know exactly who Jesus is. The humans are still figuring it out.

Jesus leaves the synagogue and goes to Simon's house. Simon's mother-in-law is suffering from a high fever. They ask Jesus to help her. He stands over her, rebukes the fever, and it leaves her. She gets up immediately and begins serving them. At sunset, people bring the sick to Jesus — all who have various diseases. He lays his hands on each one and heals them. Demons come out of many, shouting: you are the Son of God! But he rebukes them and won't allow them to speak, because they know he is the Messiah. Jesus heals individually — hands on each person. It's not a mass event; it's personal attention, one by one.

At daybreak Jesus goes to a solitary place. The people search for him and try to keep him from leaving. He tells them: I must proclaim the good news of the kingdom of God to the other towns also, because that is why I was sent. And he keeps preaching in the synagogues of Judea. The crowds want to keep Jesus for themselves — the same territorial instinct Nazareth had. Jesus refuses to be contained. His mission is wider than any single town.
